answer:
**Old English Elegies: *The Wanderer***  
The strongest single image of grief for a lost warband and hall fuses in the ruined feasting-place: "wind-blown walls stand, covered with frost, storm-battered dwellings. The wine-halls crumble, their lords lie deprived of joy, the veteran band all fell, proud by the wall."[^1] This evokes retainers slain in battle—taken by war, birds, wolves, or earth—leaving empty benches where mead once flowed amid treasure-giving lords.[^2]

**Iqbal: *Bang-e-Dara Part 3*, "Bilad-e-Islamiya"**  
Iqbal's grief for the Muslim civilizational arc centers on sacred soil turned ruin: "How could the earth of this *ruined garden* not be sacred ground? / This land is the sanctum of the majesty of Islam," where "in this dust sleep the sovereigns of the Best of Nations."[^3] Delhi, Baghdad, Cordoba evoke Hijazi glory reduced to tombs, with forebears' blood "asleep" in particles.[^4]

Both griefs are recoverable, though differently. The Wanderer's speaker explicitly imagines comfort: "Well is it for him who seeks grace, / comfort from the Father in heaven, where for us all stability stands."[^5] Earthly bonds (lord, warband, hall) are illusory loans from Wyrd and God; true consolation is patient wisdom and eternal divine stability.[^6] Iqbal's communal lament, voiced in *Shikwa* and *Jawab-e-Shikwa* (elsewhere in the corpus), rejects passive despair for revival: the ummah must awaken from "slumber," reclaim selfhood (*khudi*), and renew Hijazi fire.[^7] His grief spurs action—youth addressed to inherit ancestors' imperial spirit—making comfort imaginable as resurgence, not mere heavenly deferral.[^8] The elegist's is more inwardly "recoverable" via faith alone; Iqbal's demands collective striving, yet both pivot from loss to renewal.

[^1]: *Old English Elegies*, "The Wanderer," [translation/](https://falsafa.corpus/works/unknown-old-english-elegies-d5fe9e/11-the-wanderer/translation/#p-9462cc).
[^2]: Ibid., [translation/](https://falsafa.corpus/works/unknown-old-english-elegies-d5fe9e/11-the-wanderer/translation/#p-9462cc).
[^3]: Allama Iqbal, *Bang-E-Dara Part 3*, "Bilad-e-Islamiya," [translation/](https://falsafa.corpus/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-3-cc61e8/02-bilad-e-islamiya/translation/#p-4ecfd3).
[^4]: Ibid., [translation/](https://falsafa.corpus/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-3-cc61e8/02-bilad-e-islamiya/translation/#p-12bbc7).
[^5]: *Old English Elegies*, "The Wanderer," [translation/](https://falsafa.corpus/works/unknown-old-english-elegies-d5fe9e/11-the-wanderer/translation/#p-5bdc65).
[^6]: Ibid., [translation/](https://falsafa.corpus/works/unknown-old-english-elegies-d5fe9e/11-the-wanderer/translation/).
[^7]: Allama Iqbal, *Bang-E-Dara Part 3*, "Dil se jo baat...," [translation/](https://falsafa.corpus/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-3-cc61e8/04-dil-se-jo-baat-nikalti-hai-asar-rakhti-hai/translation/#p-420816); "Khitab ba Jawanan-e-Islam," [translation/](https://falsafa.corpus/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-3-cc61e8/13-khitab-ba-jawanan-e-islam/translation/).
[^8]: Ibid., "Bilad-e-Islamiya," [translation/](https://falsafa.corpus/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-3-cc61e8/02-bilad-e-islamiya/translation/#p-876603).

args.query: "warband" OR "mead-hall" OR "lord's hall" OR "empty hall" OR "fallen lord"
"islam" (ruin OR decline OR fall OR lost OR decay OR slumber)
